title: Listening - Part 4: Illustrating Children's Books
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with a woman called Aisha, who illustrates books for children. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: Today we’re meeting Aisha Rahman, who illustrates books for children. Aisha, were you always someone who drew pictures? || Aisha: Yes, although I never believed it could become a career. I studied graphic design and spent my first job making advertisements for furniture. Then my niece asked me to draw pictures for a story she had written about a lost penguin. [24]Her teacher displayed our little handmade book in class, and the children’s delighted reactions made me consider illustration professionally[/24]. Until then, I had drawn mainly to satisfy adult clients. || Presenter: What is the first step when you illustrate a new book? || Aisha: I receive the text, but I don’t immediately start drawing finished scenes. I read the story repeatedly and sketch ideas in a notebook. [25]I try to decide what the pictures can add that the words have not already told the reader[/25]. If a sentence says a boy is sad, there’s no need merely to draw a sad boy; perhaps the picture can show what he misses. || Presenter: Is drawing animals easier than drawing children? || Aisha: I used to think so, because animals can be simplified. Then I worked on a book about a fox family and discovered that readers watch animal expressions closely. [26]Whatever the subject, the hardest thing is making a character look recognisable and emotionally believable from page to page[/26]. A character cannot suddenly change height or personality because I drew in a hurry. || Presenter: Do publishers ask for many changes? || Aisha: They do, and that used to upset me. On one early project, an editor asked me to remove most of the detail from a busy bedroom scene. I thought she disliked my work. But [27]when I saw the simplified illustration beside the text, I realised the important objects were much easier for young readers to notice[/27]. Good editing can improve pictures. || Presenter: Are digital tools important in your work? || Aisha: I still start with pencil because I enjoy the physical movement. Later I scan the drawings and add colour digitally. [28]Using a computer means I can try different colour combinations without having to redraw the entire picture[/28]. That is particularly useful when the mood of the story changes from one page to the next. || Presenter: Do you meet the children who read your books? || Aisha: Sometimes I visit schools. Young readers are wonderfully direct; they will tell you immediately if a character looks strange. [29]Their questions often draw my attention to small story details that adults involved in producing the book have overlooked[/29]. I return to my studio with new ideas after every visit. || Presenter: What advice do you give young artists? || Aisha: Draw constantly, but also read widely. [30]An illustrator needs to understand how a story develops, not just how to make a beautiful individual image[/30]. A picture book works because words and images carry the reader forward together.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: What made Aisha think about becoming a children's book illustrator?

options.A: Her niece asked her to study graphic design.
options.B: Children enjoyed a handmade book she had drawn.
options.C: An advertising client requested characters for children.

questions.explanation: She was encouraged by children's positive reaction to the book made with her niece.

questions.question: What does Aisha consider when she first reads a story?

options.A: How her pictures can contribute new information.
options.B: Which scenes will be easiest to draw.
options.C: How closely the author expects her to follow each sentence.

questions.explanation: She tries to add something through the images rather than simply repeat the words.

questions.question: What does Aisha say is most difficult about illustrating characters?

options.A: Making animal characters attractive to adult readers.
options.B: Choosing characters suitable for different age groups.
options.C: Keeping their appearance and feelings convincing throughout.

questions.explanation: She says characters must remain recognisable and emotionally believable on each page.

questions.question: What did Aisha learn after an editor requested changes?

options.A: Extra detail is needed when books have little text.
options.B: Simpler pictures can make key things clearer.
options.C: Editors usually prefer pictures made digitally.

questions.explanation: After details were removed, the important objects stood out more clearly.

questions.question: Why does Aisha use digital tools?

options.A: To experiment with colour without beginning again.
options.B: To avoid producing pencil drawings at all.
options.C: To send sketches to schoolchildren more easily.

questions.explanation: Digital colour allows her to try alternatives without redrawing the image.

questions.question: What does Aisha value about school visits?

options.A: Children often suggest titles for future books.
options.B: Teachers tell her which books are selling well.
options.C: Children notice aspects of stories adults may miss.

questions.explanation: She says their questions highlight small details overlooked by adults.

questions.question: What does Aisha advise young illustrators to do?

options.A: Focus primarily on improving drawing technique.
options.B: Learn how storytelling works as well as drawing.
options.C: Begin with books containing very few words.

questions.explanation: She says an illustrator must understand how a story develops.
